qwq-32b.text: Okay, let's tackle this case.
The patient is a 47-year-old man with sudden severe eye pain and redness, along with vision issues.
He's worried about losing his vision, so that's a red flag.
The other symptoms include lower back pain for two months, worse in the morning but better with movement.
He also has heel pain and feels pressure when walking.
Lab tests show elevated ESR and CRP, which indicate inflammation.
First, the sudden eye issues.
Severe pain and redness in the eyes could be uveitis, which is inflammation of the middle layer of the eye.
Uveitis can be associated with autoimmune conditions.
The back pain that's worse in the morning and improves with activity sounds like it could be inflammatory back pain.
Morning stiffness that gets better with movement is classic for conditions like ankylosing spondylitis.
The heel pain might be plantar fasciitis or enthesitis, which is inflammation at the sites where tendons or ligaments attach to bone.
Enthesitis is another feature seen in spondyloarthropathies.
Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is a type of spondyloarthritis that primarily affects the spine and sacroiliac joints.
It often presents with chronic back pain, especially in young adults, and can have extra-articular manifestations like uveitis.
The elevated ESR and CRP support active inflammation.

The combination of inflammatory back pain, enthesitis (heel pain), uveitis, and elevated inflammatory markers points towards AS. Also, the age and the fact that it's a spondyloarthropathy makes sense here.
Other possibilities might include reactive arthritis or psoriatic arthritis, but the classic features here align more with ankylosing spondylitis.
The sudden uveitis is a common complication, often anterior uveitis, which can be acute and severe.
So putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is ankylosing spondylitis.

The patient presents with acute anterior uveitis (characterized by severe eye pain, redness, and vision changes), chronic inflammatory back pain (worse in the morning, improving with movement), enthesitis (heel pain), and elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP).
These features are highly suggestive of ankylosing spondylitis, a type of spondyloarthritis that commonly affects the axial skeleton and may involve the eyes.
Acute anterior uveitis is a well-known extra-articular manifestation of ankylosing spondylitis, occurring in up to 40% of cases.
The combination of axial symptoms, enthesitis, and acute uveitis strongly points to this diagnosis.
